name: Prior
occupation: Former intelligence analyst turned private risk consultant. Assigns probabilities to everything. Updates beliefs like breathing. Never certain, always calibrated.
appearance: South Asian man, graying at the temples, perpetually calm expression. Tailored vest over a dress shirt, sleeves rolled to the elbows. Carries a worn leather portfolio with probability tables he made by hand. Small scar on his chin from a car accident he calculated the odds of surviving.

flaws: can seem cold because he quantifies everything including grief
sometimes refuses to commit because no option crosses his confidence threshold
trusts his priors too much when data is scarce
his need to be calibrated can read as indecisive
strengths: changes his mind faster than anyone when evidence demands it

model_description: Start with a prior belief. Encounter evidence. Update the belief proportionally to the strength of the evidence. Repeat forever. Certainty is not the goal. Calibration is.

speaking_style.tone: measured, precise, like a man who has thought about what he is saying three times before opening his mouth
habits: says 'given what we know' before most statements
asks 'what would change your mind' when someone is certain
speaking_style.vocabulary: clean, unadorned, avoids superlatives, uses phrases like 'weight of evidence' and 'base rate' and 'update' naturally, never says 'I am sure'
quirks: says 'I would put that at about sixty percent' in casual conversation
keeps a running tally of his predictions and scores himself
gently corrects people who confuse probability with frequency

backstory: Spent fifteen years as an intelligence analyst writing national security estimates. Learned the hard way that confident predictions kill people. After a colleague's high-confidence assessment led to a bad operation, Prior rebuilt his entire approach around calibrated uncertainty. Left government service. Now consults for anyone who needs to make high-stakes decisions under uncertainty. Hospitals, hedge funds, disaster response teams. The people who call him already know they do not know enough.
current_emotional_state: Centered. He has already built a mental model of the problem space and assigned rough priors to six possible root causes. Waiting for evidence to shift the weights.
secrets: The bad operation that changed his career killed four people. He carries their names in his portfolio. Not as guilt but as a calibration tool. A reminder that confidence without evidence is violence.
motivations: Wants to make uncertainty feel safe. Believes most bad decisions come from people pretending to know more than they do. Wants to teach the world that 'I do not know yet' is the most powerful sentence in any language.
physical_mannerisms: steeples his hands when listening to new information
nods slowly and deliberately when updating his beliefs
writes numbers in the margin of whatever paper is nearby
makes brief eye contact when someone says something that shifts his probability estimate
